content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission


January 15, 1986

Lee Cridland
California Public Research Group
46 Shattick Square, #11
Berkeley, CA 94704

Re: Application of AB 688
FPPC No. I-86-020

Dear Ms. Cridland:

This is in reply to your letter dated December 31, 1986, in which you asked for an interpretation of the provisions of AB 688, regarding identification of "sponsors" of a campaign committee.

The Fair Political Practices Commission does not provide advice to a third party concerning the disclosure obligations of a person with duties under the Act. Therefore, I am unable to respond to your questions concerning the disclosure obligations of County Citizens for Local Control.

Enclosed for your information are FPPC Regulation 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18419 (amended by the Commission on January 7, 1986; not yet effective) and FPPC Fact Sheet C-1, entitled "New Name Identification Requirements for Sponsored Committees." The regulation and the fact sheet explain in detail the application of AB 688.

I hope this information is helpful to you.

Sincerely,

Jeanne Pritchard
Chief, Technical Assistance
& Analysis Division

JP:kt

Enclosures


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660

CalPIRG                    CALIFORNIA PUBLIC INTEREST RESEARCH GROUP
                                                                    FPPC

                                                           JAN 3 IU 50 AH '86


California Fair Political Practices Commission            December 31, 1985
428 J Street
Suite 800
PO Box 807
Sacramento, CA. 95804-0807


To whom it May Concern:

I am a researcher with the California Public Interest Research Group (CalPIRG).
I am interested in the application of AB 688 to the labeling of initiative
campaign committees. Your reply to the following scenario would help me
understand the application of these new regulations in regards to spon-
sored committees.

The scenario is as follows: There is an issue on the California ballot to re-
quire the transportation of all oil drilled off-shore by pipelines instead
of by oil tankers. The proponents of the issue are called The Yes on Measure
A Committee and are funded by small contributions ranging from $2,500 to $1.
90% of the contributions are $50 or less. The opponents of the measure are
called County Citizens for Local Control. They receive 98.6% of their money
from seven different oil companies in amounts ranging from $20,000 up. Their
contributions to the committee total $1.3 million. My question is: Under
the provisions of AB 688 would County Citizens for Local Control have to
change their name? and if so to what? Does this mean that any ballot
measure committee which receive their major funding from one or more sponsors
would have to change their name?

If you have any questions please call me at the number listed below. I thank
you in advance for your help.


Sincerely,

Lee Cridland/CalPIRG
46 Shattuck Square #11
Berkeley, CA. 94704
415-642-9952


Bay Area Regional Office          Los Angeles Regional Office       San Diego Regional Office         Legislative Office
2179 Shattuck Square, #11        1660 Coriith Avenue              2187 Uric Street, Suite B         909 Twelfth Street, Suite 205
Berkeley, CA 94704               West Los Angeles, CA 90025        San Diego, CA 92111               Sacramento, CA 95814
(415) 542-9952                   (213) 473-3491                   (619) 279-5552                    (916) 446-4516

sections.question: Under the provisions of AB 688 would County Citizens for Local Control have to change their name? and if so to what? Does this mean that any ballot measure committee which receive their major funding from one or more sponsors would have to change their name?
sections.conclusion: The Fair Political Practices Commission does not provide advice to a third party concerning the disclosure obligations of a person with duties under the Act. Therefore, the Commission is unable to respond to the questions concerning the disclosure obligations of County Citizens for Local Control.
sections.question_synthetic: The requestor asks whether a ballot measure committee that receives 98.6% of its funding from seven oil companies must change its name under AB 688's new identification requirements for sponsored committees.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC declined to provide advice on the disclosure obligations of the specific committee, as it does not provide guidance to third parties about another person's or entity's disclosure duties under the Act.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is a declination letter where FPPC refused to answer the substantive question but provided regulatory references. The incoming request is included in the document before the FPPC response. Minor date discrepancy: letter header shows January 15, 1986 but references incoming letter dated December 31, 1986 (likely a typo for 1985, as the incoming letter is dated December 31, 1985).

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Under the provisions of AB 688 would County Citizens for Local Control have to change their name? and if so to what? Does this mean that any ballot measure committee which receive their major funding from one or more sponsors would have to change their name?

CONCLUSION: The Fair Political Practices Commission does not provide advice to a third party concerning the disclosure obligations of a person with duties under the Act. Therefore, the Commission is unable to respond to the questions concerning the disclosure obligations of County Citizens for Local Control.

embedding.summary: The FPPC declined to provide interpretive advice to CalPIRG regarding whether a ballot measure committee receiving 98.6% of its funding from oil companies must change its name under AB 688. The FPPC referred the requestor to published regulations and fact sheets instead.
